original_hypothesis.title: Performance-fee basis can truncate to uint128 and brick executeWithdrawal or miscompute fees for large withdrawals
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In requestWithdrawal(), the per-withdrawal cost basis (withdrawalBasis) is computed as uint256 then added into a uint128 field (PendingWithdrawal.basis) via an unchecked narrowing conversion. If withdrawalBasis (or cumulative basis across requests in an epoch) exceeds 2^128-1, it will truncate, corrupting fee accounting and potentially causing executeWithdrawal() to revert due to underflow when subtracting the prorated withdrawnBasis from the (truncated) pendingWithdrawal.basis.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect: narrowing casts of value-bearing accounting fields can break invariants (basis_conservation) and create stuck withdrawals. Even if 'extreme values' are unlikely, vaults managing large TVL or high-decimal tokens can approach these bounds over time.

proof_of_signal.pattern: queuedWithdrawal[...] = PendingWithdrawal({ amount: pendingWithdrawal.amount + shares, basis: uint128(pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis) }); // uint256->uint128 truncation

preconditions: userBasis[user] * shares / userBalance (or cumulative pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis) exceeds type(uint128).max
User later calls executeWithdrawal for that epoch (or manager cancels then executes across epochs)
No compensating bounds-check exists elsewhere for PendingWithdrawal.basis growth

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: narrowing cast of accounting value (basis) to uint128
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: basis truncation can cause basisRemaining = pendingWithdrawal.basis - withdrawnBasis to underflow or mischarge performanceFee

analysis_questions: Can pendingWithdrawal.basis realistically exceed 2^128-1 given protocol constraints and intended TVL limits?
Does executeWithdrawal ever subtract a withdrawnBasis that can exceed the truncated pendingWithdrawal.basis (causing revert)?
Are there any implicit caps (e.g., uint126 TVL invariant) that make this unreachable on mainnet deployments?
validation_requirements: Property test with large basis values to observe truncation effects
Fuzz requestWithdrawal/executeWithdrawal with large balances and shares to detect underflow reverts
Check any documented/assumed caps vs. actual enforced caps in code

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker (or whale) accumulates large basis and requests a withdrawal sized so that withdrawalBasis overflows uint128. The stored pendingWithdrawal.basis truncates, then executeWithdrawal computes withdrawnBasis from the (also truncated) basis, potentially reverting on basisRemaining underflow or resulting in incorrect performance fees (systematically undercharging).
prerequisites: Sufficiently large userBasis and share balance (high TVL user or high-decimal underlying)
Epoch fulfillment occurs so executeWithdrawal is callable

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: stuck withdrawals (loss of liquidity) and/or significant performance-fee misaccounting for large positions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Large depositors; potentially entire vault if a large user becomes unexecutable and blocks operational flows off-chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low on-chain; would require manager intervention (cancel/rollover) which may still fail if truncation persists

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing boundary tests around uint128 storage and basis accumulation

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: high TVL scenario; can be simulated in tests without real capital
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain events show WithdrawalRequested/Executed but not basis truncation; requires state inspection

validation_result.mechanism: unsafe narrowing cast of accounting value (uint256 → uint128)
validation_result.impact_summary: Explicit uint256→uint128 narrowing cast on PendingWithdrawal.basis silently truncates in Solidity 0.8.x, corrupting fee accounting if userBasis accumulates past type(uint128).max. Practically requires astronomically large cumulative deposits but is a definitive code defect.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: In requestWithdrawal(), the withdrawalBasis is computed as uint256 from `(previousBasis * shares) / userBalance`. This value is then added to the existing pendingWithdrawal.basis (uint128) and the sum is stored via explicit cast: `basis: uint128(pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis)`. In Solidity 0.8.x, explicit type conversions from larger to smaller types do NOT revert—they silently truncate higher-order bits. This is distinct from arithmetic operations which DO have overflow protection. The same pattern appears in executeWithdrawal's rollover logic where `uint128(nextQueuedWithdrawal.basis + basisRemaining)` is used. If basis is truncated, performance fee calculation in executeWithdrawal computes `int256(assetsToWithdraw) - int256(withdrawnBasis)` using the corrupted (lower) basis, resulting in artificially inflated profit and overcharged performance fees. userBasis is stored as uint256 and accumulates without bound through repeated executeDeposit calls and _transferBasis operations. While each individual increment is bounded by uint128 (since deposit amounts are uint128), the cumulative total can theoretically exceed uint128 over many epochs.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies lines 138-159 of HypoVault.sol. The narrowing cast `uint128(pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis)` is present. Solidity 0.8.28 does not revert on explicit narrowing conversions—per the Solidity docs: 'If an integer is explicitly converted to a smaller type, higher-order bits are cut off.' The same unsafe pattern also exists in executeWithdrawal's rollover at the `basis: uint128(nextQueuedWithdrawal.basis + basisRemaining)` line.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: withdrawalBasis is uint256 computed from userBasis[msg.sender] (also uint256). The explicit cast uint128(...) silently truncates if the sum exceeds type(uint128).max. Solidity 0.8.x does NOT revert on explicit narrowing casts—only arithmetic operations have overflow checks.
Same narrowing cast pattern in the rollover logic. basisRemaining is uint256 and could exceed uint128 if the original basis was already large. The truncation compounds across epochs.

userBasis is uint256 and accumulates unboundedly across multiple deposit executions. This is the source of the large basis values that can eventually exceed uint128 when moved to PendingWithdrawal.basis.

calling_sequence: 1. User repeatedly calls requestDeposit/executeDeposit over many epochs, accumulating userBasis[user] as uint256
2. userBasis[user] grows past type(uint128).max through cumulative deposits (or transfers via _transferBasis)
3. User calls requestWithdrawal(shares) where shares == balanceOf[user]
5. basis: uint128(pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis) silently truncates to lower 128 bits
6. On executeWithdrawal, withdrawnBasis is computed from truncated basis → artificially low
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: requestWithdrawal reads userBasis[msg.sender] (uint256, line 144), computes withdrawalBasis as uint256 (line 146), then stores via uint128() explicit cast (line 153). The cast uses Solidity's explicit conversion semantics which truncate without reverting in 0.8.x. In executeWithdrawal, the truncated pendingWithdrawal.basis is used for fee calculation (line 242-245), producing incorrect withdrawnBasis and thus incorrect performanceFee.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The defect is inherent to the Solidity code and does not depend on configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A user (or whale) who has accumulated > 2^128 underlying token units in userBasis through repeated large deposits across many epochs would trigger silent truncation on requestWithdrawal. The truncated basis in PendingWithdrawal causes executeWithdrawal to compute a smaller withdrawnBasis, resulting in a larger perceived 'profit' and an overcharged performance fee. In extreme cases (basis truncated near zero), almost the entire withdrawal amount is treated as profit. Practically, this requires cumulative deposits exceeding ~3.4e38 token units, which is unrealistic for tokens with standard supplies. However, the code defect is real: no SafeCast or require() guard prevents the silent truncation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The code has a confirmed unsafe narrowing cast that silently truncates basis accounting values. If triggered, users would be overcharged on performance fees (basis appears lower → profit appears higher). In theory, partial fulfillment rollovers through executeWithdrawal also carry the same truncation risk, potentially compounding the corruption across epochs. The practical likelihood is very low for standard ERC-20 tokens, but the missing safeguard represents a real code defect per secure coding standards.
recommended_mitigations: Use OpenZeppelin SafeCast library: `basis: SafeCast.toUint128(pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis)` to get explicit revert on overflow
Alternatively, add `require(pendingWithdrawal.basis + withdrawalBasis <= type(uint128).max)` before the assignment
Apply the same SafeCast pattern to all uint128 narrowing casts in executeWithdrawal rollover logic and fulfillment functions
Consider using uint256 for PendingWithdrawal.basis if gas costs allow, since userBasis is already uint256
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy HypoVault with a mock ERC-20 with 0 decimals (to minimize required amounts). 2. Use vm.store to set userBasis[user] to type(uint128).max + 1. 3. Set balanceOf[user] to some value and call requestWithdrawal. 4. Observe that PendingWithdrawal.basis is truncated. 5. Fulfill the withdrawal epoch and call executeWithdrawal. 6. Verify performance fee is incorrectly calculated based on truncated basis.
uncertainties: The practical trigger requires cumulative deposits exceeding 2^128 underlying token units, which is infeasible for all known ERC-20 tokens with realistic total supplies
Whether any token with sufficiently small unit value and high total supply could approach these bounds in production
The exact Solidity version behavior of explicit narrowing casts—while documentation states truncation, some compilers may behave differently with optimizations (via_ir = true)
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with vm.store to inject large userBasis values and verify truncation behavior
Property test: assert(uint128(x) == x) for all values stored as PendingWithdrawal.basis
Integration test with mock tokens having 0 decimals to minimize required deposit amounts
Verify Solidity 0.8.28 with via_ir optimization does not add implicit overflow checks on explicit casts
